meta.name: makefile
meta.title: Makefile Skill
meta.description: Best practices for authoring GNU Make Makefiles Triggers on: **/Makefile, **/makefile, **/*.mk, **/GNUmakefile

original_definition.skill_description: Best practices for authoring GNU Make Makefiles Triggers on: **/Makefile, **/makefile, **/*.mk, **/GNUmakefile

actions.name: Place the default goal (primary build target) as the first r
actions.description: Place the default goal (primary build target) as the first rule in the makefile

actions.name: Define variables at the top of the makefile before rules
actions.description: Define variables at the top of the makefile before rules

actions.name: Define variables with `:=` (simple expansion) for immediate
actions.description: Define variables with `:=` (simple expansion) for immediate evaluation, `=` for recursive expansion

actions.name: The example below shows a pattern rule that compiles objects
actions.description: The example below shows a pattern rule that compiles objects into an `obj/` directory. The directory itself is listed as an order-only prerequisite so

actions.name: Start every recipe line with a tab character (not spaces) un
actions.description: Start every recipe line with a tab character (not spaces) unless `.RECIPEPREFIX` is changed

actions.name: Keep recipes readable; break long commands across multiple l
actions.description: Keep recipes readable; break long commands across multiple lines with backslash continuation

actions.name: Use phony targets for actions like `clean`, `install`, `test
actions.description: Use phony targets for actions like `clean`, `install`, `test`, `all`

actions.name: Use backslash-newline (`\`) to split long lines for readabil
actions.description: Use backslash-newline (`\`) to split long lines for readability

actions.name: Be aware that backslash-newline is converted to a single spa
actions.description: Be aware that backslash-newline is converted to a single space in non-recipe contexts

actions.name: Generate header dependencies automatically rather than maint
actions.description: Generate header dependencies automatically rather than maintaining them manually

actions.name: Use compiler flags like `-MMD` and `-MP` to generate `.d` fi
actions.description: Use compiler flags like `-MMD` and `-MP` to generate `.d` files with dependencies

actions.name: Use `$(error text)` or `$(warning text)` functions for build
actions.description: Use `$(error text)` or `$(warning text)` functions for build-time diagnostics

actions.name: Test makefiles with `make -n` (dry run) to see commands with
actions.description: Test makefiles with `make -n` (dry run) to see commands without executing

actions.name: $(error "gcc is not installed or not in PATH")
actions.description: $(error "gcc is not installed or not in PATH")

rules.description: Use `.PHONY` to declare targets that don't represent files
Use variables to avoid duplication and improve maintainability
Use order-only prerequisites (after `|`) for directories and dependencies that shouldn't trigger rebuilds
Include all actual dependencies to ensure correct rebuilds
Combine related commands with `&&` or `;` on the same line when they must execute together
Always declare phony targets with `.PHONY` to avoid conflicts with files of the same name
Include generated dependency files with `-include $(deps)` to avoid errors if they don't exist
Validate required variables and tools at the beginning of the makefile

directives.description: Instructions for writing clean, maintainable, and portable GNU Make Makefiles. These instructions are based on the [GNU Make manual](https://www.gnu.o
Write clear and maintainable makefiles that follow GNU Make conventions
Use descriptive target names that clearly indicate their purpose
Keep the default goal (first target) as the most common build operation
Prioritize readability over brevity when writing rules and recipes
Add comments to explain complex rules, variables, or non-obvious behavior
Name your makefile `Makefile` (recommended for visibility) or `makefile`
Use `GNUmakefile` only for GNU Make-specific features incompatible with other make implementations
Use standard variable names: `objects`, `OBJECTS`, `objs`, `OBJS`, `obj`, or `OBJ` for object file lists
Use uppercase for built-in variable names (e.g., `CC`, `CFLAGS`, `LDFLAGS`)
